coredata.dc:title: Tumor associated antigens combined with carbon dots for inducing durable antitumor immunity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Zhang, Tao
Zheng, Min
Xie, Zhigang
coredata.dc:description: Although therapeutic nanovaccines have made a mark in cancer immunotherapy, the shortcomings such as poor homing ability of lymph nodes (LNs), low antigen presentation efficiency and low antitumor efficacy have hindered their clinical transformation. Accordingly, we prepared advanced nanovaccines (CMB and CMC) by integrating carbon dots (CDs) with tumor-associated antigens (B16F10 and CT26). These nanovaccines could forwardly target tumors harbouring LNs, induce strong immunogenicity for activating cytotoxic T cells (CTLs), thereby readily eliminating tumor cells and suppressing primary/distal tumor growth. This work provides a promising therapeutic vaccination strategy to enhance cancer immunotherapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tumor lysates
Cancer immunotherapy
Antigen
Nanovaccine

Analysis of the immune activation capacity of nanovaccines
In vivo tumor suppression assay in mice
TNF-α and IL-6 in the serum of mice
Nanovaccines for the treatment of in situ and distal tumors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The nanovaccine migrates to the LNs, is absorbed by DCs, induces immature DCs to mature and present the antigen to T cells. T cells are activated to reach site of the tumor and inhibit tumor growth.

(a) SDS-PAGE protein strip analysis. (1: CM; 2: CT26-Ag; 3: B16F10-Ag; 4: CMC; 5: CMB). (b) Cell survival rate of DC2.4 cells incubated with CMB and CMC. (n = 3 separate samples). (c) CLSM images of DC2.4 cells treated with CMB or CMC for 4 h.

(a) CD80 and CD86 expression (CD11c cells as gating). (b) TNF-α and (c) IL-6 level secreted by DC2.4, after co-incubation with PBS, CDs, CM, B16F10-Ag, CT26-Ag, CMB, CMC, and LPS. Data are presented as mean ± SD (ns: not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001, n = 3).

Immunofluorescence images of (a) CD80 (b) CD86 in DC2.4 cells in the different groups. (Blue: DAPI; Green: a: CD80; b: CD86) (c) Expression of G-actin in DC2.4 from different groups. (Blue: DAPI; Green: MFs). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Accumulation of (a) CMB and (b) CMC in LNs, after injected subcutaneously with IR780 labeling CMB or CMC.

(a) Schedule of immune models. (b, d) Percentage of CD80+ CD86+ DCs in LNs. (CD11c+ cells as gating). (c, e) Proportions of CD4+CD8+ T cell in Spleens. (CD3+ cells as gating). Data are presented as mean ± SD (ns: not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001, n = 3).

(a) Treatment schedule in C57BL/6 mice carrying B16F10 tumors. Mouse tumor volume growth curve (b) and (c) tumor weight. (d) Average weight curve over time. (e) Pictures of tumors. (f) Proportion of CD4+CD8+ T cell in LNs. (CD3+ cells as gating). Concentrations of (g) TNF-α and (h) IL-6 in serum. (1: PBS; 2: αPD-1; 3: CMC; 4: CMC+αPD-1; 5: CMB; 6: CMB+αPD-1). Data are presented as mean ± SD (ns: not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001, n = 5).

(a) Treatment schedule in BalB/c mice carrying CT26 tumors. (b) Mean tumor volume growth curves and (c) tumor weight. (d) The curve of weight change of mice over time. (e) The pictures of tumors mice in the groups. (f) Percentage of CD4+ CD8+ T cell in LNs. (CD3+ cells as gating). Concentrations of (g) TNF-α and (h) IL-6 in serum. (1: PBS; 2: αPD-1; 3: CMB; 4: CMB + αPD-1; 5: CMC; 6: CMC+αPD-1). Data are presented as mean ± SD (ns: not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001, n = 5).

(a) C57BL/6 mouse tumor model establishment and treatment process. (b) The curves of average weight vs time. Mean volume growth curves of in situ (c) and distal (f) tumors. Tumor weight of primary (d) and distal (g) tumors. Pictures of primary (e) and distal (h) tumors. (1: PBS; 2: αPD-1; 3: CMC; 4: CMC+αPD-1; 5: CMB; 6: CMB+αPD-1). Data are presented as mean ± SD (ns: not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001, n = 5).

(a) Proportion of CD80+ CD86+ DCs in LNs. (CD11c+ cells as gating). (b) The rate of CD4+ CD8+ T cells in tumors. (CD3+ cells as gating). (c) Quantification diagrams of (a and b). (d) TNF-α and (e) IL-6 in serum (1: PBS; 2: αPD-1; 3: CMC; 4: CMC+αPD-1; 5: CMB; 6: CMB+αPD-1). Data are presented as mean ± SD (ns: not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001, n = 5).

head.ce:title: Tumor associated antigens combined with carbon dots for inducing durable antitumor immunity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, 126 Xiantai Street, Changchun, Jilin 130033, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, 126 Xiantai Street, Changchun, Jilin 130033, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although therapeutic nanovaccines have made a mark in cancer immunotherapy, the shortcomings such as poor homing ability of lymph nodes (LNs), low antigen presentation efficiency and low antitumor efficacy have hindered their clinical transformation. Accordingly, we prepared advanced nanovaccines (CMB and CMC) by integrating carbon dots (CDs) with tumor-associated antigens (B16F10 and CT26). These nanovaccines could forwardly target tumors harbouring LNs, induce strong immunogenicity for activating cytotoxic T cells (CTLs), thereby readily eliminating tumor cells and suppressing primary/distal tumor growth. This work provides a promising therapeutic vaccination strategy to enhance cancer immunotherapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer immunotherapy [1–7] has emerged as one of the most promising clinical strategies for the treatment of malignancies due to its decent performance of activating the adaptive antitumor immunity. Several formulations have been proposed to upregulate immunity against cancer: (1) DNA/RNA immunotherapy [8–11]; (2) cytokine immunotherapy [12]; (3) Chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) immunotherapy [13]; (4) cancer vaccine [14,15] and (5) immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) therapy [16–21]. Among the immunotherapy modalities, cancer vaccines which can effectively trigger antitumor immunological response to kill tumor cells, have shown favorable therapeutic efficacy [22,23]. However, the drawbacks such as inferior lymph node retraction capacity, insufficient antigen recognition and presentation efficiency of dendritic cells (DCs) have severely hampered immunogenicity and long-term immunity.

Cancer nanovaccines with nanovehicle-loaded immunomodulators can induce potent antitumor immunity, which is a kind of promising immunotherapeutic approach. Nanovectors [24–33] can not only enhance the bioavailability of antigens, but also improve the efficiency of antigen cross-presentation, which plays a vital role in cancer immunotherapy. Furthermore, to induce more durable specific immune responses, cell-derived nanovaccines are constructed by loading tumor cell lysates with nanovectors, which can improve the immunogenicity of nanovaccines to contain massive tumor-associated antigen (TAA).

In previous studies, we have demonstrated that carbon dots (CDs) can serve as therapeutic nanocarriers [34,35], due to their ultra-small size, good biocompatibility, excellent cell permeability [36,37], and high specific surface area. In view of this, we prepared cell-derived nanovaccines to specifically target lymph nodes, facilitate tumor antigens presentation and infiltration of DCs to inhibit tumor growth and metastasis. The nanovaccines (named as CMB and CMC) were prepared by the conjugation of CDs with tumor lysates, respectively. In addition, the cancer nanovaccines were synergised with ICB (αPD-1) to achieve an enhanced therapeutic effect (Scheme 1 ). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic investigation of CDs-based cell-derived nanovaccines for the inhibition of primary and distant tumors.

Citric acid (99.5 % purity) purchased from Beijing Yili Fine Chemicals Co. Polyethyleneimine 25 k (99 % purity) purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. 3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) (98 % purity) was purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Co. The BCA kit was purchased from Bainite Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). SDS-PAGE gel rapid preparation kit and color pre-stained protein molecular weight standards (10–180 kD) were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) was purchased from Source Leaf Biotechnology Co. Rabbit Anti-Calreticulin Polyclonal Antibody (bs-5913R), Rabbit Anti-HMGB1 Polyclonal Antibody (bs-0664R) and Donkey Anti-Rabbit IgG H&L/FITC (bs-0295D-FITC) were purchased from Bioss Biotechnology Ltd (Beijing, China). Rat Anti-mouse CD80/PE Monoclonal Antibody (bsm-30264A-PE) and Rat Anti-mouse CD86/FITC Monoclonal Antibody (bsm-30162A-FITC) were purchased from Bioss Biotechnology (Beijing, China). FITC anti-mouse CD3 (Clone Number: 17A2), PE anti-mouse CD4 (Clone Number: RM4-5) and APC anti-mouse CD8a (Clone Number: 53-6.7) were purchased from DAKEWE Biotechnology Co. Annexin V-FITC/PI double-stained apoptosis detection kit (Clone Number: KGA108), mouse TNF-α (Clone Number: KGEMC102a-1) and IL-6 (Clone Number: KGEMC004-1) ELISA kits were purchased from KGI Biotechnology Co. CD80/B7-1 Mouse Monoclonal antibody (Catalog Number: 66406-1-lg), CD86 Rabbit Polyclonal antibody (Catalog Number: 13395-1-AP), Fluorescein (FITC)-conjugated Affinipure Goat Anti-Mouse IgG (H+L) (Catalog Number: SA00003-1) and Fluorescein (FITC)-conjugated Affinipure Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG (H+L) (Catalog Number: SA00003-2) were purchased from Proteintech (Wuhan, China).

A transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was performed on JEOL JEM-1011 (Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum was performed on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were performed on the LS-55 fluorophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs were recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer between 4000 and 500 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained by Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Multitechnique surface analysis. Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd.) was used to analyze the Zeta potential of the nanoparticles. CLSM images were performed on the Zeiss LSM 780 imaging system. The flow cytometry measurement was performed on a Becton Dickinson FACS Aria sorting flow cytometer (Becton-Dickinson, USA). Mouse lymph node imaging was performed with a PerkinElmer IVIS Lumina Seres Ⅲ in vivo imaging system.

Citric acid (CA) (0.6 g) and PEI (25 k) (0.5 g) were dissolved in 10 mL H2O. The mixture was then transferred to an autoclave with a Teflon liner and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Finally, the crude product was cooled to room temperature and the solution was placed in a dialysis bag (Mw cut-off: 3.5 KDa) and dialyzed against water for 10 h to remove unreacted small molecules. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs in 36.4 % yield.

21.12 mg (0.1 mmol) of maleimidic caproic acid, 46 mg (0.2 mmol) of NHS and 80 mg (0.2 mmol) of EDC were dissolved in 3 mL of THF under stirring. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 8 h to activate the carboxylic acid groups. 20 mg of CDs were dissolved in 3 mL of methanol, respectively. After that, CDs were added to the mixture and the reaction was continued for 12 h. After the reaction, a precipitate was generated and centrifuged at 3500 r/min for 3 min. The supernatant was taken and dialyzed for 8 h. The solution was freeze-dried to obtain CM.

To prepare tumor-derived protein antigens from tumor cells, tumor cells (B16F10, CT26) were inoculated in a culture dish. After incubation overnight, TPP NPs (25 µg mL−1) were added to the culture dishes and incubated for 4 h. B16-F10, CT26 cells were then washed with PBS and irradiated with a 635 nm (1.0 w/cm2) laser for 5 min. Subsequently, B16-F10, CT26 cells were incubated with 1640 medium without FBS for 48 h. Finally, the supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 2000 r/min for 5 min to remove the non-tumor-derived protein antigen. Finally, the supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 2000 r/min for 5 min to remove insoluble cell debris, which is the tumor antigen.

CM solution (2 mg mL−1, 1 mL) was incubated with 2 mL of tumor antigen for overnight in a 37 °C incubator. After 24 h of incubation, the solution was collected and placed in an ultrafiltration tube and centrifuged (2000 rpm, 5 min), then washed with PBS twice to remove free antigen. The final product was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator.

B16F10 and CT26 were inoculated into 96-well plates separately and incubated with TPP NPs (25 µg mL−1) for 4 h. Cells in each well were then illuminated using a 635 nm laser for 5 min and incubation was continued for 3 h. The medium was then aspirated and discarded, and each well was washed three times with 100 μL of PBS, followed by the addition of 100 μL of paraformaldehyde to fix the cells for 20 min. Afterwards, paraformaldehyde was aspirated and washed three times with PBS, and 150 μL of BSA was added to each well to seal the cells at room temperature for 1 h. Then the BSA was aspirated and washed three times with PBS, and 150 μL of primary antibody was added to each well and incubated overnight in a 4 °C refrigerator. After overnight, the primary antibody was aspirated and recovered, and each well was washed three times with PBS for 3–5 min each time, followed by the addition of 150 μL of secondary antibody and incubation at 4oC for 1 h. Finally, the secondary antibody was aspirated and recovered, and PBS was added three times. Add 150 μL of Hoechst to each well and incubate for 5 min protected from light. The coverslip containing the cells in each well was removed and slowly placed on a slide with a drop of glycerol, then the excess glycerol was blotted with filter paper, and both sides of the coverslip were coated with clear nail polish and observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

B16F10 and CT26 were inoculated into 96-well plates separately and incubated with TPP NPs (25 µg mL−1) for 4 h. Cells in each well were then illuminated using a 635 nm laser for 5 min and the incubation was continued for 3 h. The medium was then aspirated and discarded, and each well was washed three times with 100 μL of PBS, followed by the addition of 100 μL of paraformaldehyde to fix the cells for 20 min. After that, paraformaldehyde was aspirated and discarded, PBS was added three times, then 200 μL TritonX-100 was added to each well and incubated for 5 min to disrupt the cell membrane and enhance cell permeability, after which Triton X-100 was aspirated and discarded, 150 μL BSA was added to each well to seal the cells at room temperature for 1 h. BSA was then aspirated and washed three times with PBS, and 150 μL of primary antibody was added to each well and incubated overnight in a four-degree refrigerator. After overnight, the primary antibody was aspirated and recovered, and each well was washed three times with PBS for 3–5 min each time, followed by the addition of 150 μL of secondary antibody and incubation at 4 oC for 1 h. Finally, the secondary antibody was aspirated and recovered, washed three times with PBS, and incubated for 1 h at four degrees. Add 150 μL of Hoechst to each well and incubate for 5 min protected from light. The coverslip containing the cells in each well was removed and slowly placed on a slide with a drop of glycerol, then the excess glycerol was blotted with filter paper, and both sides of the coverslip were coated with clear nail polish and observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

DC2.4 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates with different concentrations of CDs and nanovaccines and incubated for a total of 24 h. Then 20 μL of MTT was added to each well, and after 4 h, 150 μL of DMSO was added to each well. The absorbance of each well was measured at 490 nm using an enzyme marker.

DC2.4 cells were inoculated in six-well plates with CDs (50 μg mL−1), CM, CMB and CMC (50 μg mL−1) and incubated for 4 h. Cells in each well were centrifuged and the supernatant was discarded. Cells were then washed three times with PBS, and 1 mL of paraformaldehyde was added to each well to fix the cells for 10 min, after which the paraformaldehyde was aspirated and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Nucleation solution (Hoechst, 1 mL) was added to each well and incubated for 5 min at room temperature. The nucleation solution was then aspirated and washed three times with PBS. The coverslip containing the cells in each well was removed and slowly placed on a slide with a drop of glycerol, then the excess glycerol was blotted with filter paper, and both sides of the coverslip were coated with clear nail polish and observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

DC2.4 cells were inoculated in a six-well plate, and then CDs (50 μg mL−1), CM, CMB and CMC (50 μg mL−1) and incubated for 12 h. The cells in each well were centrifuged (1500 rpm, 8 min), and the supernatant was collected to determine tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α) and interleukin 6 (IL-6) by ELISA. Then the cells were stained with PE-CD80 and FITC-CD86 antibodies for 1 h. Cells were closed with 5 % BSA for 0.5 h, washed with PBS, and centrifuged at 1800 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was discarded and 0.5 mL of PBS was added to resuspend the cells. The expression of CD80 and CD86 on DCs was measured by flow cytometry.

DC2.4 was inoculated into 96-well plates and incubated with CDs (50 μg mL−1), CM, CMB and CMC (50 μg mL−1) were incubated for 12 h. The medium was then aspirated and discarded, and each well was washed three times with 100 μL of PBS, followed by the addition of 100 μL of paraformaldehyde to fix the cells for 20 min. Afterwards, paraformaldehyde was aspirated and washed three times with PBS, and 150 μL of BSA was added to each well to seal the cells at room temperature for 1 h. Then the BSA was aspirated and washed three times with PBS, and 150 μL of primary antibody was added to each well and incubated overnight in a four-degree refrigerator. After overnight, the primary antibody was aspirated and recovered, and each well was washed three times with PBS for 3–5 min each time, followed by the addition of 150 μL of secondary antibody and incubation in a 4-degree refrigerator for 1 h. Finally, the secondary antibody was aspirated and recovered, and PBS was added three times. Hoechst (150 μL) was added to each well and incubate for 5 min protected from light. The coverslip containing the cells in each well was removed and slowly placed on a slide with a drop of glycerol, then the excess glycerol was blotted with filter paper, and both sides of the coverslip were coated with clear nail polish and observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the ability of nanovaccines to target lymph nodes
ce:section.ce:para: Nanovaccines were labelled with IR780, then subcutaneously injected into the back of Balb/c mice and the lymph nodes were imaged by a PerkinElmer IVIS Lumina Seres Ⅲ in vivo imaging system at different time points.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the immune activation capacity of nanovaccines
ce:section.ce:para: Mice were injected subcutaneously with the nanovaccines on the day 0, 5, 10 and 15 and continued to be observed for three days before being executed and the spleen and lymph nodes were collected for flow analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tumor suppression assay in mice
ce:section.ce:para: On day 0, B1F10 cells (10 × 105 cells/100 μL) were injected into the back of C57BL/6 mice and CT26 cells (20 × 105 cells/100 μL) were injected into the back of Balb/c mice to establish a tumor model. When the tumor volume reached approximately 80 mm3, mice were injected subcutaneously with the nanovaccine once every 5 d for a total of 4 times. The dose was 3 mg kg−1 nanovaccine per mouse. The mice were given intraperitoneal injection of αPD-1 at a dose of 2.5 mg kg−1 on the second day of the dosing. The body weight and tumor size of mice were monitored every three days. After treatment, mice were executed, tumors and major organs were collected.

On day 0, B1F10 cells (10 × 105 cells/100 μL and 5 × 105 cells/100 μL) were injected into the right and left sides of the back of C57BL/6 mice to establish in situ and distal tumor models, respectively. When the in situ tumor volume reached approximately 80 mm3, the mice were given subcutaneous injection of the nanovaccine every 5 d for a total of 4 doses. The dose was 3 mg kg−1 nanovaccine per mouse. The mice were given intraperitoneal injection of αPD-1 at a dose of 2.5 mg kg−1 on the second day of the dosing. The body weight and tumor size of mice were monitored for every three days. After treatment, mice were executed, tumors and major organs were collected.

After execution of the mice, tumors, spleens and lymph nodes were collected and immediately examined for T cells and DC cells in them. Organ tissues were ground, washed with PBS, centrifuged and the cells stained using antibodies. Cells were then washed with PBS and analysed by flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TNF-α and IL-6 in the serum of mice
ce:section.ce:para: After treatment, blood was collected from the submandibular vein of mice. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and the expression of TNF-α and IL-6 in mouse serum was analyzed by ELISA.

In order to evaluate the safety and anti-tumor effects of different treatment groups, major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) and tumors were collected, and these tissue sections were stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) for pathological analysis.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal route using citric acid (CA) and polyethylenimine (PEI: 25 k) as precursors. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) reveals that the diameter of CDs is 31.7 nm (Figure S1). The UV–Vis absorption spectra show that CDs has three peaks at 213, 244 and 354 nm (Figure S2). When the excitation wavelength is 460 nm, the maximum emission of CDs appears at 550 nm (Figure S2). The full-scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of CDs indicates the presence of three elements (C, N, and O) (Figure S3a). The HR-XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Figure S3b) was resolved into 284.8 (CC/CC), 286.7 (CN/CO) and 287.6 (CO) eV. The two peaks (398.9 and 400.8 eV) in the HR N 1 s spectrum (Figure S3c) are associated with NH and CN groups. The HR O 1s spectrum was fitted into two peaks (530.5 and 532.2 eV), corresponding to CO and CO, respectively (Figure S3d).

CM was prepared by the conjugation of maleimide caproic acid with CDs (Scheme 1), which has spherical morphology and good dispersion with diameter of 39.6 nm (Figure S4). The hydrodynamic diameter (HD) of CM measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Figure S5) is 60.62 nm. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CM exhibited two peaks (220 and 347 nm) (Figure S6). The optimum excitation and emission wavelength is 340 and 458 nm, respectively. The zeta potential of CDs and CM is + 12.3 and + 11.7 mV, respectively. The XPS spectra of CM (Figure S7) are similar to those of CDs. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs (Figure S8) shows the presence of the flowing groups: –OH/–NH (3368 cm−1), CH (2944, 1403, 1209, and 999 cm−1). A new peak appeared at 1697 cm−1 (CO stretching of amide) (Figure S8), indicating that CM was successfully synthesized. The cytocompatibility of CDs and CM was evaluated against DC2.4 cells (Figure S9) and all of the cell viabilities were ∼100.0 %, demonstrating their superior cytocompatibility. DC2.4 cells were cultured with CDs and CM for 4 h each and then subjected to confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) to examine the internalization of CDs and CM. Figure S10 shows that more intense green fluorescence appears in the CM group, indicating that CM is more easily internalized by DC2.4 cells.

Tumor antigens derived from photothermal therapy-induced immunogenic cell death (ICD) of B16F10 and CT26 tumor cells using tetraphenylporphyrin nanoparticles (TPP NPs) as a photothermal agent. B16F10 or CT26 tumor antigens were incubated with CM for overnight to prepare nanovaccines (CMB and CMC). The antigen content in CMB and CMC was 36.4 % and 25.7 %, respectively.

As shown in Figure S11a, CMB is uniformly distributed with diameters of 98.2 ± 4.0 nm. Figure S11b shows that CMC has a rectangular lamellar morphology of approximately 173.6 nm in length and 125.7 nm in width. The zeta potential of CMB and CMC is −3.7 and −3.6 mV, respectively (Figure S12a). The HD (Figure S12b and Figure S13) of CMB and CMC in water, PBS and 10 % serum was monitored and the almost negligible change in size indicates that both CMB and CMC has good stability within seven days. Sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) analysis (Fig. 1 a) depicts that CM has no bands. The bands in CMB and CMC correspond to B16F10 and CT26 cell-derived antigens, confirming the successful synthesis of CMB and CMC. The biosafety of CMB and CMC was verified by MTT assay. Fig. 1b shows a gradual increase in cell viability with increasing the concentration of CMB or CMC, probably because the nanovaccines stimulated the proliferation of DC2.4 cells. To confirm the above results, we injected the nanovaccines subcutaneously into the mice at a dose of 3 mg/kg, and the haematology analysis (Figure S14) indicated that all the physiological indices were within the normal range, confirming that the nanovaccines were free of toxicity at the current dose.

Since immunogenic cell death (ICD) can lead to the ectopia of calreticulin (CRT) and the release of high-mobility histone 1 (HMGB1) from cells. Therefore, we performed immunofluorescence analysis of CRT and HMGB1 in B16F10 and CT26 cells. As shown in Figure S15a and 15c, CRT was barely expressed in the PBS, TPP NPs and PBS+L groups was scarcely expressed in both cell lines. In sharp contrast, CRT was highly expressed in the TPP NPs+L group. Similarly, HMGB1 was highly released in the TPP NPs+L group (Figure S15b and 15d). Figure S16 shows the quantitative fluorescence intensity plots of CRT exposure and HMGB1 release in B16F10 and CT26 cells. These results clearly demonstrate that tumor-associated antigens can be obtained by ICD.

We evaluated the ability of nanovaccines to be taken up by DCs. After incubation with CMB and CMC for 4 h, strong green fluorescence appeared in DC2.4 cells (Fig. 1c), demonstrating that CMB and CMC could be effectively internalized by DCs.

Antigen presentation is a central to the process of immune activation. Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) can activate cytotoxic T cells (CTLs) and helper T cells (Ths). DCs are a type of APCs, whose maturation is characterized by the expression of CD80 and CD86. We therefore measured the expression of DC2.4 co-stimulatory factors such as CD80 and CD86 under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 2 a, PBS, CDs, CM, B16F10-Ag and CT26-Ag could not induce a significant upregulation of CD80 and CD86. In contrast, the expression of double positive DC cells in CMB and CMC was 44.7 and 36.8 %, respectively, and even exceeded the expression of lipopolysaccharide (LPS). This suggests that CMB and CMC can significantly stimulate the maturation of DC cells for antigen presentation. After maturation, DC cells can secrete cytokines including TNF-α and IL-6 (Fig. 2b and c). According to the standard curves (Figure S17 and S18), the concentration of TNF-α in the CMB and CMC groups was 49.1 and 41.3 pg/mL, respectively, and the concentration of IL-6 showed the same trend, which was higher than the levels of cytokines secreted by the LPS group. The above results indicate that CMB and CMC can be internalized by DC2.4 cells and promote their maturation.

Immunofluorescence assays were performed to further verify that CMB and CMC could stimulate CD80 and CD86 expression in DC2.4 cells. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, fluorescence was very weak in the PBS, CDs, CM, B16F10-Ag and CT26-Ag groups, whereas strong green fluorescence appeared in the CMB and CMC groups, indicating that CMB and CMC could indeed promote DC2.4 maturation and upregulate CD80 and CD86 expression (Fig. 3a and b). Microfilaments (MFs) are an important part of the cytoskeleton, and the G-actin monomers in MFs can recognize tumor antigens. The biological movement of MFs influences cell morphology and function. We therefore investigated the formation of G-actin in DC2.4. The fluorescence intensity in the CMB and CMC groups is comparable to that in the LPS group, suggesting that CMB and CMC could induce cells to from MFs (Fig. 3c).

CMB and CMC were labelled with IR780 dye to validate their ability to target lymph nodes (LNs). The BalB/c mice were injected subcutaneously with the IR780-labelled CMB or CMC and then the inguinal LNs of the left hind limb were imaged. It can be seen that the fluorescent signal of the LNs gradually increased after the injection of CMB, and reaching its maximum at 8 h, and then gradually decreased (Fig. 4 a). However, the signal still remained strong at 24 h, which undoubtedly proved the efficient accumulation of CMB in LNs. The accumulation process of CMC in the LNs was similar to that of CMB (Fig. 4b), with the fluorescence signal reaching its maximum at 8 h. Figure S19 shows the quantitative plot of the fluorescence signal intensity. The results indicate that CMB and CMC can improve the accumulation of LNs and prolong the retention time.

C57BL/6 mouse models were established to verify the immune activation of CMB and CMC. PBS, CDs, CM, CT26-Ag, B16F10-Ag, CMC and CMB were administered subcutaneously to the mice on the day 0, 5, 10 and 15, respectively. On day 18, the LNs and spleens were analyzed for immune activation (Fig. 5 a). Maturation of DCs in LNs was analyzed (Fig. 5b and d). The double positive DC cells in PBS, CDs, CM, CT26-Ag, B16F10-Ag, CMC and CMB groups were 8.62, 9.16, 10.8, 10.8, 11.0, 22.5 and 33.6 %, respectively, demonstrating that CMC and CMB could obviously upregulate the secretion of CD80 and CD86. Apparent proliferation of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells on LNs was detected in CMC and CMB groups, and the proportion of CD4+CD8+ T cells was 40.0 % and 44.8 %, respectively (Figure S20), demonstrating that CMC and CMB could markedly enhance T cells proliferation. The expression of T cells in the spleen was also examined (Fig. 5c and e), and the high percentage of CD4+CD8+ T cells in the CMC (34.3 %) and CMB (42.7 %) groups confirmed that they could stimulate T cell activation in the spleen.

Since CDs, CM, B16F10-Ag and CT26-Ag cannot induce immune stimulation, PBS, αPD-1, CMC, CMC+αPD-1, CMB and CMB+αPD-1 were selected as control and experimental groups to evaluate the tumor suppression and immune activation capacities of CMC and CMB. On day 0, B16F10 tumor cells (10 × 105/100 uL) were injected into the right back of C57BL/6 mice. As shown in Fig. 6 a, mice received subcutaneous injections of CMC and CMB (3 mg/kg) every five days, and αPD-1 (2.5 mg/kg) was injected intraperitoneally in the day after each vaccine injection and four times during the treatment. The tumor growth curves (Fig. 6b) showed that PBS and αPD-1 have no therapeutic efficacy, CMC and CMC+αPD-1 also failed to delay tumor progression, because CMC prepared from CT26 antigen could not target B16F10 tumors. In contrast, CMB made from B16F10 specific antigens could specifically show greater anti-tumor performance, especially after combination with αPD-1 (CMB+αPD-1), one tumor was completely eradicated (Fig. 6e). The CMB+αPD-1 group had the lowest tumor weight (Fig. 6c). The weight increased steadily throughout the treatment, indicating that CMB has acceptable biosafety (Fig. 6d).

We harvested LNs, tumors and spleens to analyse the number and phenotype of immune cells. CMB+αPD-1 could drastically upregulate the positive ratio of T cells in LNs, tumors and spleens (Fig. 6f, Figure S21 and S22). Changes in secreted cytokines were also examined. CMB+αPD-1 could induce a significant upregulation of the proportion of CD80+CD86+ lymph node resident DCs (Figure S23), whereas CMC and CMC+αPD-1 derived from CT26 antigen did not induce DC maturation, demonstrating the specificity of CMB towards B16F10 melanoma. During immune activation, a number of cytokines were secreted into the serum, so we measured the serum levels of TNF-α and IL-6 (Fig. 6g, h). Compared to the other groups, the CMB and CMB+αPD-1 groups increased the levels of TNF-α to 100.3 and 132.2 pg/mL and IL-6 to 20.8 and 41.2 pg/mL, respectively, which again confirmed the therapeutic and immune-activating functions of CMB.

Organs and tumor tissues were then sectioned and embedded for staining analysis. As shown in Figure S24, tumor cells in the PBS and αPD-1 groups were compactly distributed without lysis. However, after the treatment with CMB and CMB+αPD-1, the tumor cell nucleus was obviously lysed. In addition, the mice showed no pathological abnormalities in major organs and no tumor metastasis, further demonstrating the biosafety and therapeutic efficacy of CMB.

To further validate the specificity of CMC, we established a CT26 tumor model (Fig. 7 a). CMB and CMB+αPD-1 failed to inhibit tumor growth (Fig. 7b, c and e), whereas CMC and CMC+αPD-1 induced an apparent delay in tumor growth, with one mouse being completely cured. Body weight growth curves showed a steady upward trend (Fig. 7d). In addition, CMC and CMC+αPD-1 stimulated the proliferation of T cells in LNs, tumors and spleens (Fig. 7f, Fig. S25 and S26). The maturation ratio of DCs in LNs was also significantly increased (Figure S27). Serum cytokine levels were measured by ELISA (Fig. 7g, h). After CMB+αPD-1 treatment, TNF-α and IL-6 reached the highest levels with the concentration of 62.5 and 22.7 pg/mL, respectively. H&E staining confirmed the biosafety and therapeutic efficacy of CMC and CMC+αPD-1 (Figure S28). These results demonstrated that CMC+αPD-1 could achieve effective antitumor immunotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanovaccines for the treatment of in situ and distal tumors
ce:section.ce:para: To test whether CMB nanovaccines could induce systemic anticancer immune responses, we established in situ and distal tumor models on the left and right sides of the back of mice (Fig. 8 a). Fig. 8c and f show the average volume growth curves of in situ and distal tumors, respectively. In situ and distal tumors were almost completely suppressed in the CMB+αPD-1 group, which was significantly superior to the other groups, with tumor inhibition rate of 81.5 and 76.8 %, respectively (Figure S29). The tumor weight (Fig. 8d and g) also showed the best antitumor effect of CMB+αPD-1, with one distal tumor disappearing (Fig. 8e and h). During this period, all mice were in good condition with steady weight gain (Fig. 8b). The above data suggest that the combination of CMB and αPD-1 can delay the progression of primary tumors and induce ectopic effects, resulting in the regression of distant tumors.
H&E staining analysis confirmed that CMB+αPD-1 significantly lysed tumor cells and had no toxic effects on major organs in mice (Figure S30).

At the end of the treatment, the proportion of CD80+CD86+ DCs in the LNs and T cells in the major tissues was analyzed. The maturation rate of DCs reached 34.5 % after CMB+αPD-1 treatment, which was significantly higher than that in the other groups (Fig. 9 a and c). The percentage of CD4+CD8+ T cells in the tumor tissue of the CMB+αPD-1 group was 42.9 %, indicating that a large number of T cells had migrated to the tumor site (Fig. 9b, c). CMB+αPD-1 also significantly upregulated the proportion of T cells in LNs and spleens (Figure S31 and S32). Serum cytokines levels were measured (Fig. 9d and e). Compared to the other groups, the CMB and CMB+αPD-1 groups upregulated the levels of TNF-α (80.9 and 115.5 pg/mL) and IL-6 (18.2 and 28.5 pg/mL).

CM were synthesized by the surface modification of CDs with maleimidohexanoic acid, two tumor cell-derived antigens (B16F10-Ag, CT26-Ag) were prepared by photothermal induction of immunogenic cell death, and nanovaccines (CMB and CMC) were fabricated by the conjugation of CM with B16F10-Ag and CT26-Ag, respectively. In vitro experiments showed that CMB and CMC had excellent biosafety and could be taken up by DC2.4 cells for antigen presentation. In vivo experiments demonstrated CMB and CMC could target lymph nodes, induce DC cells maturation, upregulate co-stimulatory factor levels, and activate T cells. Furthermore, both CMB and CMC can induce a systemic immune response and inhibit the growth of tumors, which has higher immunotherapeutic efficacy compared to previous work [17,23,24]. CMB and CMC could inhibit the growth of primary tumors and distal tumors. Especially, after the combination with αPD-1, the inhibition rate of CMB+αPD-1 on B16F10 in situ and distal tumors reached 81.5 and 76.8 %, respectively. Therefore, this therapeutic vaccination regimen may offer valuable prospects for the construction of personalised vaccines for cancer immunotherapy.

Hongxin Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Tao Zhang: Methodology. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhigang Xie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), and the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).
This work has received approval (No.2022-0006) for research ethics from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.06.101.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:
